blog/bifurcation-and-inter-contract-enfo.$value:
The cost of authoring a bounded
abstraction is falling toward zero, so the cost moves somewhere else
Each piece is internally fine

The industry hand-maintains
the same command four times — CLI, MCP tool, OpenAPI route, tool schema — and
hopes they stay in sync

What it can't do yet, what nobody has really
solved, is keep all of those honest with each other while it keeps editing them

Hand an agent a crisp contract and it fills in the
implementation, the tests, the docs

A contract no tool enforces is
not a standard; it's a wish, and an agent regresses it the first time it's
convenient

The door checks an owner and a policy,
acts or refuses, and records either way:

It's the problem this whole
 project is aimed at
What's a result and what's still a bet
I grade my own claims against the running code, so plainly:

When agents write most of the software, the scarce resource is trust between
the pieces, not authoring

That
boundary is exactly what makes an agent useful on it

The filesystem door assumes one ownership model; the process door has already
drifted to another

- Enforced today — the drift gates (docs from source) and executable behaviour
 specs

If they break, the build breaks.
- Partial — "privileged effects attributable to a signed owner." True on
 the common path; the named gap is a permission boundary I'm still closing.
- The bet — contracts staying honest between components as they evolve

The process spawner and tool
adapters, the CLI — all of it
One sanctioned door per kind of power

They don't. prx derives them from one spec, so the
surfaces can't disagree: there's only one thing to disagree with

The interesting
failure mode is the seam
Same door, one allow, one deny, both recorded
In agent-built software, the open problem is the seams between the pieces
That gap is the whole problem, and it's where I work
prx is my answer: an argument I make in running code

That's the
bet, and I'm working it in the open, graded against the code
Drift is a build failure, not a code review

The unsolved problem is keeping many bounded, agent-authored abstractions
honest against each other as they evolve — enforcing contracts between ideas,
not just within one

Agentic development works by bifurcation: a good idea earns its own bounded
abstraction with a defined contract. "Filesystem access" becomes a thing with
edges — a name, a surface, a promise about what it does and doesn't do
Authoring is getting cheap

That
 one is stated as direction, not a finished result

So docs generate from source and fail CI on drift, and behaviour
specs execute against the engine
An agent can write the filesystem layer now
The seam is where that trust is won or lost

The capability-seam idea converges with established object-capability and
information-flow research; that mapping is a later post

Trust is mechanical — gates on every change,
not reviewer vigilance that can't scale to agent throughput
The bet: three mechanisms for the seams

Meanwhile the CLI says one thing and its MCP adapter says a
slightly older thing

The seam is the only path, so the
seam is where authority is decided
The unsolved part is between the abstractions, not inside them

For each ambient capability there is
exactly one way through — a door you hold a socket to, never the keys behind it:
fs, proc, env, gh, git, cas
Most tooling secures a single action

You can scope an agent to a
contract; you can't scope one to a vibe
One source of truth, projected to many surfaces

When authoring is cheap, you get more of it: more seams, more contracts, and
that many more places two agent-authored pieces can quietly come to disagree

blog/provenance-is-not-legitimacy.md#015.$value: - Enforced — at deploy time a manifest of the whole site is keyless-signed, and the built site ships to GHCR as a signed OCI artifact; both Rekor entries are public. cosign verify-blob plus sha256sum -c confirms identity and integrity of the live bytes; cosign verify checks the pulled artifact

blog/provenance-is-not-legitimacy.md#089.$value: The token is consumed at build time and gone; the certificate lives for one build and expires

blog/provenance-is-not-legitimacy.md#0a5.$value: It never proved the build should have happened

blog/provenance-is-not-legitimacy.md#0b3.$value: The badge links the entry; /provenance.json carries the pointer

blog/provenance-is-not-legitimacy.md#139.$value: Provenance proved the package was built in a particular CI environment

blog/provenance-is-not-legitimacy.md#148.$value: The signing pipeline lives in deploy.yml; the per-deploy record is at /provenance.json

blog/provenance-is-not-legitimacy.md#17a.$value: The people who oversell the green badge are the foil

blog/provenance-is-not-legitimacy.md#1de.$value: The signature and its inclusion proof land in Rekor, the public append-only transparency log

blog/provenance-is-not-legitimacy.md#2f2.$value: It mints an OIDC token at runtime, submits it to Fulcio, and the provenance it produces passes every standard check: the badge is green, the certificate subject is a real CI identity, the Rekor entry is genuine — and the package still steals credentials

blog/provenance-is-not-legitimacy.md#32d.$value: The first cut signed one asset — a single stylesheet — on the theory that one signed file carries the whole argument

blog/provenance-is-not-legitimacy.md#32f.$value: That gap is small, easy to oversell, and the entire point

blog/provenance-is-not-legitimacy.md#36f.$value: A log entry is not trust by itself, either: the strength of a transparency log is not that it blocks bad entries, but that it makes them monitorable

blog/provenance-is-not-legitimacy.md#381.$value: A signed build tells you who produced an artifact and that the bytes are intact

blog/provenance-is-not-legitimacy.md#401.$value: The security shows up only when someone watches the log for their own identity appearing when they did not publish — which is, notably, one of the few controls that actually caught the worm

blog/provenance-is-not-legitimacy.md#4d1.$value: Trust comes from you checking the log, not from the badge existing

blog/provenance-is-not-legitimacy.md#51c.$value: There is no native way for a page to prove its own legitimacy to a visitor, and identity is not legitimacy

blog/provenance-is-not-legitimacy.md#580.$value: Closing that second gap takes a different mechanism: a capability boundary that checks each privileged action as it happens — which is the rest of what Bounded Systems is for

blog/provenance-is-not-legitimacy.md#585.$value: We do not build a registry — Rekor is the per-version registry, and we push to it directly with cosign sign-blob

blog/provenance-is-not-legitimacy.md#588.$value: No stored key (the OIDC identity is the key)

blog/provenance-is-not-legitimacy.md#5eb.$value: The badge attests origin, not authority

blog/provenance-is-not-legitimacy.md#629.$value: Here is the ladder it sits on, and the ceiling it stops at

blog/provenance-is-not-legitimacy.md#641.$value: Put precisely: this is authentication, not authorization, and the two are orthogonal

blog/provenance-is-not-legitimacy.md#69f.$value: Both are graded against the code that backs them.

blog/provenance-is-not-legitimacy.md#6be.$value: That proves the deployed bytes are a pure function of source anyone can read

blog/provenance-is-not-legitimacy.md#6db.$value: One sha256 line per file, keyless-signed and shipped at /site.sha256

blog/provenance-is-not-legitimacy.md#6e2.$value: It does not tell you the build was meant to happen

blog/provenance-is-not-legitimacy.md#6e5.$value: But the honest unit of provenance for a website is the website: a visitor wants to trust the page they are reading, not one file on it

blog/provenance-is-not-legitimacy.md#6f1.$value:
If signing breaks, the deploy step fails.
- Partial — the badge is a claim we render, and your browser does nothing with it

blog/provenance-is-not-legitimacy.md#700.$value: In CI, the GitHub Actions OIDC token authenticates to Fulcio, which mints a signing certificate bound to the repository, workflow, and commit

blog/provenance-is-not-legitimacy.md#70e.$value: Authentication asks who — and a signature checked against a transparency log answers that statically, after the fact

blog/provenance-is-not-legitimacy.md#715.$value: Two mechanisms, each earning its place:

blog/provenance-is-not-legitimacy.md#7a2.$value: This site now signs its entire built output at deploy time — a content-addressed manifest of the whole site, plus the site itself pushed to a registry as a pullable artifact

blog/provenance-is-not-legitimacy.md#7f4.$value:
The verify recipe is right there so you can; most visitors will not.
- Not claimed — that the build is safe or authorized

blog/provenance-is-not-legitimacy.md#800.$value: So the scope is the whole built output

blog/provenance-is-not-legitimacy.md#846.$value: A visitor verifies the signature once, then checks the live bytes with sha256sum -c

blog/provenance-is-not-legitimacy.md#892.$value: The badge in the proof section is the result, and it is scoped on purpose: it says produced by this identity, not verified safe

blog/provenance-is-not-legitimacy.md#920.$value: What survives is the attestation: this site was signed by this identity

blog/provenance-is-not-legitimacy.md#961.$value: The build is a Nix derivation: nodejs and the brand are pinned by flake.lock, so nix build.#site yields the same dist/ on any machine

blog/provenance-is-not-legitimacy.md#9cc.$value: It says nothing about who ran the build

blog/provenance-is-not-legitimacy.md#a30.$value: A build can be impeccably authenticated and still act outside what it was ever meant to

blog/provenance-is-not-legitimacy.md#ad8.$value: A signed version becomes one entry, keyed to the artifact digest, queryable by anyone

blog/provenance-is-not-legitimacy.md#b84.$value: Three rungs, each proving a different thing, none of them the next

blog/provenance-is-not-legitimacy.md#bea.$value: Why the whole site, and where it stops

blog/provenance-is-not-legitimacy.md#c09.$value: That is the whole pipeline: reproducible proves matches public source; OIDC plus Sigstore proves who built it; Rekor is the immutable per-version record of those proofs

blog/provenance-is-not-legitimacy.md#c1a.$value: There is no key to store, leak, or rotate

blog/provenance-is-not-legitimacy.md#cc0.$value: Authorization asks what this is allowed to do — a question about behavior at runtime that no signature observes

blog/provenance-is-not-legitimacy.md#ce4.$value: None of it proves the build was authorized

blog/provenance-is-not-legitimacy.md#d18.$value: A recent supply-chain worm made this concrete

blog/provenance-is-not-legitimacy.md#d1b.$value: No third signing mechanism "for completeness." The discipline is not sign as little as possible — it is sign the honest unit, justify each mechanism, and stop when the next one would be ceremony

blog/provenance-is-not-legitimacy.md#eb4.$value: So the honest reading of any green badge is narrow

blog/provenance-is-not-legitimacy.md#f0e.$value: The built site pushed to GHCR and cosign-signed by digest, so the exact bytes are fetchable and versioned, not just rebuildable

blog/provenance-is-not-legitimacy.md#f62.$value: The careful claim — provenance, not legitimacy — is the differentiator, and it is the same instrument-versus-finding discipline this whole project runs on: state what the mechanism proves, name what it does not, and grade it against the running code

blog/trust-lives-outside-the-page.md#710.$value:
- Reproducible (Nix) — the deployed bytes are a function of public source.
- Identity (keyless OIDC + Sigstore) — who built it, no stored key.
- Registry (Rekor) — an immutable, public, per-version record of those proofs.
- Monitoring (rekor-monitor) — because a record nobody watches is not a control.
- The ceiling — none of it proves the build was authorized

blog/trust-lives-outside-the-page.md#80c.$value: This is the sequel to provenance is not legitimacy

blog/trust-lives-outside-the-page.md#839.$value: Each rung proves a different thing, and naming what each one does not prove is the point — not a disclaimer bolted on at the end

blog/trust-lives-outside-the-page.md#89e.$value: An unwatched entry is a receipt nobody reads

blog/trust-lives-outside-the-page.md#8d3.$value: It's turtles: the validator only runs because the page chose to load it — and chose its hash

blog/trust-lives-outside-the-page.md#944.$value: The page is grading its own homework

blog/trust-lives-outside-the-page.md#a57.$value: Builds here ship a /rekor link to this version's actual entry on search.sigstore.dev

blog/trust-lives-outside-the-page.md#a7c.$value: If we lied about which entry, the digest there wouldn't match the one we serve — so the lie is detectable, not hidden

blog/trust-lives-outside-the-page.md#a8b.$value: That genuinely helps — it shrinks the trusted surface from "arbitrary inline script" to "this one pinned, auditable artifact." But it doesn't escape the trap, because the page still chooses which script to load and which hash to pin

blog/trust-lives-outside-the-page.md#b08.$value: So the honest question is not "how do I verify inside the page," but "what can the page not forge."

blog/trust-lives-outside-the-page.md#b24.$value: A tampered page swaps the bytes and the verifier, and the check goes green anyway

blog/trust-lives-outside-the-page.md#b63.$value: - The CLI you run yourself — cosign verify-blob against the published bundle, then sha256sum -c

blog/trust-lives-outside-the-page.md#b67.$value: A scheduled job follows the public log for our build identities and opens an issue the moment a certificate is minted for us that we didn't trigger

blog/trust-lives-outside-the-page.md#ba9.$value: The verifier you can run yourself is verify-site; this build's log entry is one click away at /rekor

blog/trust-lives-outside-the-page.md#bfa.$value:
The page can't reach into your shell.
- A browser extension you installed — verifies regardless of page script, because you chose it.
- A third-party monitor watching the log on your behalf.
- In CI: admission control (policy-controller) that refuses anything not signed by an allowed identity — outside any single artifact's say-so

blog/trust-lives-outside-the-page.md#c74.$value: Neither asks you to trust this page.
A verifier a page ships is also a claim the page controls — if the bytes can lie, the script that checks them can lie too

blog/trust-lives-outside-the-page.md#cf9.$value: A badge a page renders is a claim the page controls

blog/trust-lives-outside-the-page.md#d11.$value: The supply-chain worm from earlier this year minted its own OIDC token at runtime and produced provenance that passed the standard checks — green badge, real CI identity, genuine Rekor entry — while stealing credentials

blog/trust-lives-outside-the-page.md#d78.$value: State what the mechanism shows, name what it can't, and put the trust where the page can't reach

blog/trust-lives-outside-the-page.md#d83.$value: A log nobody watches proves nothing

blog/trust-lives-outside-the-page.md#d9b.$value: SRI only checks the bytes match the hash you declared; it doesn't make the page trustworthy for declaring it

blog/trust-lives-outside-the-page.md#dc4.$value: So we now run that watch

blog/trust-lives-outside-the-page.md#dce.$value: The transparency log's whole value is that it can be watched — and watching is the publisher's job, not Sigstore's

blog/trust-lives-outside-the-page.md#e04.$value: The reflex fix is Subresource Integrity plus a provenance-backed CDN: load the verifier from a third party, pin it by hash, and now the page can't swap the validator

blog/trust-lives-outside-the-page.md#e88.$value: users are responsible for monitoring the log for unauthorized certificates issued to their identities … if no third parties monitor the logs, then any misbehaviour might go undetected

blog/verbspec-author-once-project-everyw.$value:
The surfaces aren't generated-then-edited; they're computed from the spec on
demand

If you maintain a CLI and an MCP server and an OpenAPI spec for the same
operations, this collapses four hand-maintained artifacts into one — and the one
can't lie to the other three.

verbspec is the projection layer — author once, project everywhere,
drift-free

A verb's input and output are Zod schemas — the single
source of truth, giving you runtime validation and static types from one
definition. z.toJSONSchema turns it into the interchange IR, and every surface
is a pure function of that IR:
Get the CLI, the MCP tool, the OpenAPI route, and the model schema for free

Four artifacts, four
hand-maintained copies of the same shape — and they drift

That's
the whole trick: with a single definition, drift has nothing to occur between
You author the verb once:

This is one mechanism from a larger bet — keeping agent-authored contracts
honest as they multiply (see the door is the unit of bounded
authority). verbspec is the
piece with the most pull on its own.

The
capability/security story is separate; that's the door work. verbspec is
MIT, on npm, with zod the only peer dependency

The help text says
one thing, its tool schema says a slightly older thing, the OpenAPI spec a
third

The package is a clean leaf: its only production dependency is the zod peer
dep, and an extractability test enforces that it stays pure — outward-only
imports, no ambient authority (no shelling out, no process.env)

It handles one job: the "one source of truth projected to many
surfaces" mechanism, pulled out as a standalone, usable thing
There's nothing to keep in sync because there's only one thing

No codegen and no
build step — and no FFI to author a verb

…and the CLI (greet Ada --loud), the MCP tool, the model schema, and
POST /greet are all just that spec seen from different sides

Ship one capability to both humans and agents today and you write it four times:
a CLI with flag parsing and --help, an MCP tool definition, an OpenAPI
operation, and a tool-use schema for Anthropic's models

The fix is boring and total: there should be one definition, and every
surface should be a pure projection of it

You can drop
it into anything
One spec, four surfaces
Each rots on its own schedule, and nothing tells you when

blog/why-i-built-this-agent-kept-doing-t.$value:
Six months ago the project was dev-contracts — I wanted to give an agent a
contract it couldn't drift away from

It writes code, runs
commands, and opens pull requests

A solo project, worked out in the open and graded against
the running code — but the first user it has to convince is me

It edits a file I didn't mean to touch, runs a
command I wouldn't have run, or quietly does three extra things on the way to the
one I actually asked for

It became prx when what actually
mattered turned out to be driving whole units of work, not holding a single
contract

It
scopes what the agent can reach: the job gets exactly the capabilities it needs
and nothing else
Why I built this: my coding agent kept doing things I didn't ask for

This post is the plain version of
what these tools are and how I got here — usage first, because that's the part
you can't see from the repos

When something comes out wrong, I can see which step it came
from.

If a tool here
doesn't earn its keep in my own daily work, it doesn't belong in the project
Same problem, three names

That sentence is the whole project, and until now it was nowhere on
the page
I run an AI coding agent on my own work every day

And
prx grew guest-room when I needed the agent's authority to be the thing
under contract: one sanctioned way to reach each kind of power, instead of
trusting the agent to stay in its lane
Each one is the last one seen more clearly
guest-room is the part I built most recently, and the one I reach for now

If you're chewing on the same thing — an agent that's useful but won't stay in
bounds — I'd like to talk.

That's what keeps the agent from wandering — it can't get into
what it was never handed
Bounded Systems is what I built to stop that
Two things, used together.
None of this showed up as a plan
That loop is the point

The short version, the one that actually lands when I say it out loud:
guest-room is what I did so the agent didn't get distracted or do things I
didn't want

I build these because they help me build these. prx and guest-room are the tools
I use to work on prx and guest-room — every project is meant to make the next one
safer and faster to do with an agent

It's all derivations of one need, renamed each
time I understood it a little better

It earns its place — and it has a habit I got
tired of: left alone, it wanders
Why I keep building it

I hand it an issue ticket and it chains the pull
requests from there, writing structured output at each step — so the path from
"here's the task" to "here's the PR" is a sequence of artifacts I can check, not
one opaque jump

It's also the honest answer to "is this a real thing or an idea?" It's real
because I depend on it

guest = your agent

 room = the doors it holds

Aspirational 
 prx and claude-box converge onto one guest-room door runtime. — guest-room v0 ships the model (rooms, doors, attenuation, confinement); prx still runs a parallel in-process door stack

index.html#929e50129c31.$value: For each kind of system power there is exactly one sanctioned way through — a capability seam: a door you hold a socket to, never the keys behind it

index.html#94fa33afaf95.$value: Hand an AI coding agent a real task and it reaches past it — touching files you didn't mean to expose, running a command you didn't intend

Claude Code, plugged into a room 
 The first guest: a Claude Code runtime whose authority is the door references it holds — keeper, scout, concierge, net — and nothing beyond them

 in-toto · SLSA · DSSE

Enforced 
 guest-room's behaviour specs execute against the engine

index.html#a98e04f4f31e.$value: Bounded Systems scopes that down: each kind of system power — git, the shell, your environment, your tools — gets one sanctioned access point that checks your policy, acts or refuses, and records what happened

index.html#ad5ec81a63e7.$value:
The libraries, as a graph 
 Every capability is a typed node; the edges are how they compose

index.html#aecaa15715c2.$value: Bounded Systems works there: privileged effects are attributable to a signed owner, one source of truth projects to many surfaces, and drift is caught as a build failure, so the contracts can't quietly rot

index.html#af71e79828a2.$value: Git-writes carry a signed in-toto / SLSA derivation — a per-actor ed25519 signature over content-addressed inputs and outputs, recorded in a derivation ledger

index.html#aff660b39d0c.$value:
The capability model, as a runnable library 
 A guest-agnostic capability runtime: rooms, doors, append-only attenuation, lease-bound confinement

index.html#b36357ca3849.$value: One narrow seam per kind of ambient system power — the single sanctioned access point in prx today, each one a door on the guest-room runtime tomorrow — so effects stay attributable and policy stays enforceable

index.html#b54400a836d4.$value: Keep it inside the job you gave it

index.html#b6eef77a81e1.$value:
Partial 
 A git-write carries a verifiable in-toto / SLSA provenance derivation — signed per-actor, content-addressed in a derivation ledger, checked fail-closed at the merge gate. — the sign→verify loop is tested but opt-in today; gh, external reads, and egress are policy-gated, not yet signed
